stalling their commitments to move away
from fossil fuel. Um they're not going
towards renewable energy sources such as
solar or wind, for example, because
those resources right now can't sustain
the massive power consumption demand.
Big data center developers have
announced massive investments in
renewable projects like solar plants,
wind farms, or nuclear power.
For example, in Pennsylvania, Microsoft
has struck a deal to buy power from the
notorious 3mile island nuclear power
plant when it reopens in 2027. The plant
suffered a partial meltdown in 1979.
But just like with the water credits,
big tech companies are also looking to
offset their carbon footprint by paying
others to deliver renewable energy to
the grid on their behalf. The big
question is, can the already fragile and
fragmented grid supported? And if
massive infrastructure upgrades are
needed, who picks up the tab? Amazon,
Microsoft, and Google told Business
Insider they were committed to paying
their full share for upgrades to grid
infrastructure like high voltage power
lines.
But there's plenty of evidence pointing
to the fact that costs are already being
passed on to customers. In Virginia,
Dominion Energy disclosed that it would
need to roughly double its electricity
generation by 2039, mainly to meet
demand from data centers and electric
vehicles.
The expansion could cost up to $103
billion, increasing residential
electricity bills by as much as 50%.
Despite the burden on the grid and on
water supplies, some states are doing
all they can to attract data centers
into their backyards.
Many regions simply don't want to miss
out on the AI boom or the kudos of
having a big tech employer on their
doorstep. So far though, the promise of
large numbers of jobs has not
materialized. A Business Insider
analysis found that even the largest
data centers employ fewer than 150
permanent workers and some have as few
as 25.
But the tax breaks keep on coming.
>> What we found is that massive
corporations are benefiting from various
taxes in different states for their data
centers.
We tracked 37 states offering tax
incentive programs to data centers like
zero tax on building materials,
machinery or equipment, but also
preferential rates on water and
electricity. In Virginia, 56 data center
projects received tax savings of almost
a billion dollars in the 2023 fiscal
year alone.
Take a look at these data centers in New
Orle.
In 2017, a littleknown LLC called
Sidecat went to the city of New Albany
in Ohio and said, "We are going to build
two gigantic data centers on about 300
acres of land. In return, could you
please give us 100% property tax
abayments for at least 15 years?" And
the county said, "Okay." By our
estimate, Scycat LLC received at least
60 million in fork on taxes. And then,
you know, everybody found out that
Scikut LLC is not a mom and pop a data
center company, but was Meta, the parent
company of Facebook. The roll out of AI
across industries from social media to
medical care will accelerate data center
construction. In 2025 alone, Meta
planned to spend at least $64 billion on
facilities and equipment. Google's 75
billion and Microsoft 80 billion. And
the ultra powerful computer chips
driving the AI tools will consume more
and more energy. A 2024 Department of
Energy report estimates that their
electricity use, driven by the AI boom,
could soon require as much as 12% of
total US electricity use. In 2023, it
was just over 4%.

these data farms, there are decisions to
be made.

truly understand the severity of what's

and I'm hoping to be out before that

breaks my heart, but I can only fight

To be honest, I've never really run into so much resistance for records than this project. >> Big tech companies often go to great lengths to hide the details. So, we decided to build a map. >> What we designed is a system to see uh not only the individual location of each data center across the country, but also where they cluster. >> And for some, these clusters are appearing too close to home. This is Lowden County, Virginia, otherwise known as data center alley. And it may not look like much from down here, but just take a look from up above. [Music] These are Amazon data centers. Zoom out and you can see tech companies tend to build data centers in clusters where there is a reliable power supply, access to enough water, as well as tax breaks and affordable land. But of course, these tend to also be places where lots of people already live, like Santa Clara County, California, or the most heavily populated part of Arizona, Maricopa County. It was originally meant for housing, but in 2023 it was reszoned to clear the way for the construction of data centers instead. Prince William County already has over 70 data centers. Donna set up a local campaign to challenge the reasonzoning, but the lawsuit was dismissed. >> Yeah, it's heart-wrenching. It's heart-wrenching. No. There is no definitive public directory of data centers, no official map, no single regulator to ask or government agency to foyer. >> So, you know, it's it's been really tricky to kind of get these records because the companies don't want to disclose all of that information. >> By tricky, we mean redacted records and requests denied on the grounds of trade secrets. It turns out there is one thing that most data centers need. Backup generators in case the grid fails. And anyone who wants to install a generator needs to apply for an air quality permit. So what we set out to do was request all of the permits that are issued to data centers for those backup generators. >> That meant filing public record requests for air permits in every state. Take this hotspot rapidly expanding close to Columbus, Ohio. There are at least 164 emergency generators permitted here. This is a data center where the air permit was applied for by an LLC called Mellin Enterprises LLC. Um, but the company has applied for a trade secrets uh exemption. water used in cooling and

electricity generation. The 
BBC asked OpenAI for more

details about Sam Altman's 
estimate, but the company

declined. Either way, it's clear 
AI uses a lot of water. But

why? Every time you send a 
prompt to an AI, it has to run

complex calculations to understand 
and respond. This work is

done by the most powerful and 
specialised computer chips in

the world, housed inside enormous 
data centres. Even before

users can send prompts, the 
training process for the models

uses the chips to carry out 
intense work. And all that extra

power means the hardware can 
overheat and become damaged if

not cooled properly. Most 
data centres use air cooling
systems, which was fine until I came along.

But now, because these data 
centres and the infrastructure

that's going in is so much more 
energy intensive, there are

liquid cooling approaches that 
are now being implemented.

For liquid cooling the water 
must be clean to prevent

bacteria growing or clogs and 
corrosion in the system, which

means using mostly drinking 
water. Here's how the most

common liquid cooling process 
works. It begins by piping

coolant over the processing 
chips within the servers. This

cooling liquid absorbs the 
heat and takes it away from the

electrics to a heat exchange 
unit. Water is used to reduce

the temperature of the coolant. The coolant then
recirculates back to cool the servers.

Meanwhile, the now
hot water is piped

to cooling towers, where a combination of
fans and water vapour dissipate the heat,

cooling the water.
Some of the water

evaporates in that process, while the rest
is recirculated through the cooling process

several times
before being

discharged back into the nearby water source.
Overall, up to 80% of the water evaporates.

What it means is that this 
type of water is gone, and that

we are extracting water 
from a water circuit that is

necessary for irrigation, for 
human consumption and hygiene.

Communities around the world 
concerned about data centres

putting stress on water sources 
and electricity grids are

pushing back. Protests have 
been held in Spain, India,

Chile, Uruguay and parts of 
the US. And it's not just the

operations within the data centre that need water.
Generating the electricity to run

them requires a lot of
water too, because power

plants like coal, gas and nuclear
heat water to create steam,

which drives a turbine. The
International Energy Agency

has said electricity demand for
AI optimised data centres is

expected to increase by 400% by
2030 to 300 terawatt hours.

That's roughly the electricity
consumption of the whole of the

UK for a year. And aside
from electricity,

water is also needed when manufacturing
the semiconductor chips used to run AI.

So water is both used directly 
and indirectly in the whole

supply and creation chain of 
AI technologies. It is used for

the refination of the critical 
raw materials that are needed
to create the hardware of AI.

Getting accurate figures on how 
much water it takes to build

AI systems and run them is

having. Many, including Google,

pledged to be water neutral by 2030.

1 billion messages sent to  ChatGPT every day, and ChatGPT 
is just one AI bot. Chuck in  Gemini, DeepSeek, Claude and 
others. It's clear that the AI  revolution is a thirsty one. 
Striking though it is, some  experts are more than a little 
sceptical of Sam Altman's  estimate on water usage. At this point, there was just 
not enough information for me  to agree with or trust the 
number. Their number was perhaps  referring to some tiny models. 
We're considering a medium  sized large language model 
that's the size of GPT-3.  Basically, if you write an 
email or ask some questions, if  you have 10 to 50 queries, 
you're going to be consuming  roughly 500ml of water. This calculation includes 
water used in cooling and  electricity generation. The 
BBC asked OpenAI for more  details about Sam Altman's 
estimate, but the company  declined. Either way, it's clear 
AI uses a lot of water. But  why? Every time you send a 
prompt to an AI, it has to run  complex calculations to understand 
and respond. This work is  done by the most powerful and 
specialised computer chips in  the world, housed inside enormous 
data centres. Even before  users can send prompts, the 
training process for the models  uses the chips to carry out 
intense work. And all that extra  power means the hardware can 
overheat and become damaged if  not cooled properly. Most 
data centres use air cooling  systems, which was fine until I came along. But now, because these data 
centres and the infrastructure  that's going in is so much more 
energy intensive, there are  liquid cooling approaches that 
are now being implemented. For liquid cooling the water 
must be clean to prevent  bacteria growing or clogs and 
corrosion in the system, which  means using mostly drinking 
water. Here's how the most  common liquid cooling process 
works. It begins by piping  coolant over the processing 
chips within the servers. This  cooling liquid absorbs the 
heat and takes it away from the  electrics to a heat exchange 
unit. Water is used to reduce  the temperature of the coolant. The coolant then
recirculates back to cool the servers.   Meanwhile, the now
hot water is piped   to cooling towers, where a combination of
fans and water vapour dissipate the heat,   cooling the water.
Some of the water   evaporates in that process, while the rest
is recirculated through the cooling process   several times
before being   discharged back into the nearby water source.
Overall, up to 80% of the water evaporates. What it means is that this 
type of water is gone, and that  we are extracting water 
from a water circuit that is  necessary for irrigation, for 
human consumption and hygiene. Communities around the world 
concerned about data centres  putting stress on water sources 
and electricity grids are  pushing back. Protests have 
been held in Spain, India,  Chile, Uruguay and parts of 
the US. And it's not just the  operations within the data centre that need water.
Generating the electricity to run   them requires a lot of
water too, because power   plants like coal, gas and nuclear
heat water to create steam,   which drives a turbine. The
International Energy Agency   has said electricity demand for
AI optimised data centres is   expected to increase by 400% by
2030 to 300 terawatt hours.   That's roughly the electricity
consumption of the whole of the   UK for a year. And aside
from electricity,   water is also needed when manufacturing
the semiconductor chips used to run AI. So water is both used directly 
and indirectly in the whole  supply and creation chain of 
AI technologies. It is used for  the refination of the critical 
raw materials that are needed  to create the hardware of AI. Getting accurate figures on how 
much water it takes to build  AI systems and run them is 
difficult. Google, Meta and  Microsoft release annual 
figures showing that their data  centres use billions of litres 
of water every year from  local sources, but none of 
them indicate how much of it is  due to AI. Most tech giants 
recognise the impact it's  having. Many, including Google, 
Microsoft and Meta, have  pledged to be water neutral by 2030. S5
We hope that can happen,   there is a long way to go to get to
those kind of numbers. Part of what   we hope to see is,
across the industry,   a range of innovations that allow us to
maybe minimise the use of water. Companies are trialing, for 
example, ways to cool data  centres without evaporating any 
water at all. And to use the  heat that's generated to 
warm homes. There are also  experiments to move data 
centres away from communities  entirely under the sea, to 
the Arctic, or even off the  planet. Can we actually put capacity 
out in space? It's very, very  early stage. So what, you know, 
we at NTT are looking at is,  can we launch satellites that 
can at least do some more  backup-oriented or other oriented tasks? Though skeptics point to the 
many hurdles that need to be  overcome, there is optimism, 
too, about a more sustainable  future. Let's remember that that GenAI 
capability is still very,  very young. It's moved 
exponentially fast, but as an  industry and as a use, it is 
still young. Ideally, we can  learn together as as a society 
and as a world society, how  do we minimise against the use

segments.text: What happens when you ask Chat GPT
to reword an email?
Your prompt goes to a datacenter
and triggers a wave of computations
on hundreds of servers.
As those servers get hit with requests
from thousands of users at once
they start to heat up.
To keep them from overheating,
data centres typically cool them
with water, fans and air conditioners.
OpenAI doesn't publicly share exactly
how much energy or water
its models consume,
but one researcher we spoke to
put together some estimates.
For the simplest tasks,
like rewording your email,
their popular GPT-5 model
uses as much electricity
as it takes to power a three watt LED light bulb
for around 20 minutes,
and a little less than a teaspoon of water.
For one person
and one prompt
it may not seem like much.
Open AI says it receives
2.5 billion queries a day.
Add those up and at the very least
you're looking at enough water
to meet the daily drinking needs
of all the people in Monterey, Mexico,
and enough energy
to binge watch Netflix for more than 3000 years.
The number of AI users
is expected to roughly double
over the next half decade,
and as AI usage is increasing,
so too is the number of data centers
needed to support it.
That's why researchers are racing
to find new and better ways
to keep server racks cool,
like sinking data centers
deep into the ocean
and even launching them into space.
Cooling has always been a core part
of how data centers work.
Even one of the first computers
in the 1940s had vacuum tubes,
which were cooled by fans and ventilation.
Despite being literally
trillions of times more powerful,
the data centers of today
still look kind of similar.
The big difference is
they're using a lot more energy.
Nearly 50% of their entire energy use
can go towards keeping the CPUs,
that process your prompts,
also known as microchips,
from overheating.
This Google datacenter
in the US state of Oregon
is large enough to run computations
needed for AI applications.
A typical data center
covers an area of about 9000 square meters.
This one is 13 times bigger.
It's called a hyperscale data center.
"The scale of the AI data centers,
which we call Hyperscalers,
is so much larger than the data centers
that we have been living with,
pretty much without even noticing them
for the past decade plus."
That's Professor Scanlan
from the University of Wisconsin,
who wrote this paper
about the hidden environmental costs
of AI data centers.
Packing in more servers means a lot more cooling.
Google's hyperscaler in The Dalles, Oregon
uses nearly a third
of its city's water supply in 2021
according to public records
obtained by the state's largest newspaper.
Relying on water for cooling
is a common approach.
That's why when you look closely at this map,
you see big clusters of data centers here
close to freshwater.
Nearly a 10th of the world's data centers
are in this part of the US.
The region is called the Great Lakes
and is home
to one fifth of the world's surface freshwater,
much of it naturally cold.
Using it lets data centers spend
far less electricity on cooling.
The water gets sprayed
into rooftop cooling towers,
allowed to cool naturally through evaporation
and then used to keep servers from overheating.
Fewer fans and less electricity.
The method is called evaporative cooling.
The tradeoff?
A lot more water.
"Less than 1% of the water in the Great Lakes
is annually renewed
and many ecosystems,
city drinking water,
industries
rely on a relatively stable level of the lakes,
so the water that can be taken out
for use by humans –
it has to fit within that water budget."
Now let's look at the other side of this tradeoff.
Google's data center in Mesa, Arizona,
sits in one of the driest regions
in the United States.
So they went with air cooling:
Giant fans pushing air across the servers.
No water, but a lot more electricity.
"The data centers in Ireland
are consuming
a fifth of all of our electricity."
Rosie Leonard
is an environmental activist from Ireland.
"So it's really, really high.
So they are directly causing
a strain on the energy system
and the public is paying for this
through increased bills."
Research found that using evaporative
rather than air cooling
can reduce the energy needed
to cool a data center
by between 30 and 60%.
But as new AI models
drive data centers
to pack in more powerful chips
and denser servers,
they're generating more heat than ever,
meaning cooling needs to
work even harder to keep up.
Or it can work smarter.
One solution
is to directly cool the microchips,
where most heat is generated,
instead of the whole server room.
Direct-to-chip cooling
uses a water-based liquid coolant.
This circulates across metal plates -
typically made from copper or aluminum -
stuck onto the chips.
Companies say using liquid
to absorb heat from chips
in a recirculating,
closed-loop system,
cools more effectively
than chilling the air.
"Liquids move heat faster than air does...
Air is very light
and liquids are very dense...
So, with the same volume
moving through the system,
liquid can remove way more of the heat."
That's Maxie Reynolds
from the data-center company Subsea Cloud.
We'll hear more from her later.
Research found that direct-to-chip cooling
can reduce the power used by server fans
by up to 80% compared to air-based methods.
And the liquid itself
remains sealed in a closed loop.
So no on-site water consumption
is necessary to make it happen.
It looks like this method
is becoming the new norm
for large scale data center providers.
Microsoft is building what it calls its
"most powerful AI data center"
in Wisconsin,
in that same Great Lakes region.
They say this facility will house
"hundreds of thousands of processors",
and they're using direct-to-chip cooling
to save on water.
Studies by McKinsey
and Future Market Insights estimate that,
by the end of 2030,
direct-to-chip cooling buildouts
will represent
about a third of the data center cooling sector.
But what if you could
take that idea even further,
and dunk an entire server
into cooling liquid?
I know what you're thinking –
tossing an AI server rack
into a cold water bath
is a sure way to fry the system.
So, for immersion cooling to work,
data center providers
use special oily liquids,
that won't conduct electricity
or cause metal to rust.
Gigabyte,
a data center services provider in Taiwan,
says cooling its servers in this bubbling liquid
allows them to be stacked more densely.
With the direct-to-chip liquid cooling method
we explored earlier,
some fans are still needed.
With immersion cooling,
there's no air around the server,
removing the need for fans altogether
One operator in Athens, Greece
has reported that over 97% of the energy
that the data center uses goes strictly
to crunching computations
instead of cooling.
A big jump from the roughly 50%
seen in many traditional,
air-cooled data centers.
Industry researchers found that
the immersion-cooled data center sector
was already valued
at $1.7 billion US dollars in 2025.
That represents
a third of the liquid-cooled data center market
as a whole.
In the frosty harbors of Denmark,
one operator is taking 'cooling'
to a whole new level...
...by plunging data centers directly
into the frigid waters of the North Sea.
"And by placing them subsea
we eliminate the electrically driven cooling.
So we see about a 40% reduction
in the power that's consumed,
and then about 40% decrease
in the carbon emissions.
There's Maxie again.
Her company's
shipping-container-like capsules
weigh 46 tons.
Inside, high-density server racks
hold over 1,100 processors,
suspended in –
you guessed it –
a nonconductive oily coolant goo.
The heat this coolant absorbs
from the computing components
quickly emanates outward
into the frosty waters just outside.
The modules can be connected,
for example,
to offshore wind turbines
to power the computations,
though no electricity is needed for cooling.
No fans,
no lights,
and no pumps for circulating
the gooey coolant.
This means that 99% of electricity
goes directly to computing power,
according to the company.
There are problems
with underwater data centers, though.
"Every day there's some GPU
that fails due to
plenty of different reasons."
Pengfei Li is a researcher
focused on AI's environmental impact.
"If it's on the ground,
we can easily replace them
or fix the errors
on these on these GPUs
or some other hardware,
but if it's under the sea
then it's kind of hard to to maintain..."
Surveys show that over half of facilities
have experienced at least one outage
in the past three years...
Though most of these are minor hiccups,
it shows that problems can come
from all angles
like memory errors,
network glitches,
power issues,
or even natural disasters.
But there's one more data center frontier,
and this one goes far beyond
the limits of Earth itself.
Operators are exploring the idea
of shooting data centers into space...
to orbit around our planet.
The idea is
they can rely on solar-power
for their computing
while the cold of space
would help to naturally
keep the servers cool.
By staying close to Earth's surface,
data could travel back and forth
using optical lasers without too much lagtime.
Elon Musk's rocket-launch company
SpaceX
merged with his
artificial intelligence company xAI,
with the goal of launching
AI datacenters into space.
But researchers are skeptical.
"Data centers require a lot of cooling
that's actually really hard to do in space."
Samantha Lawler
is an astronomer who studies space junk.
"It seems like,
'Oh, space is cold,
infinite heat sink capacity',
but, it's actually really hard
to get the heat to leave,
right?
When the sun is shining,
all that heat builds up."
Objects in space
exposed to direct sunlight
can get as hot as 120 degrees Celsius.
And because there's no air,
that heat can't easily go anywhere.
Lawler also points out
that despite its name,
'space' is getting crowded...
With around 11,000 satellites
already in low earth orbit.
"We do need to think more carefully
about prioritizing which satellites
are most beneficial to the most people.
There are satellites
that are useful for saving human lives,
for planning crop rotations,
for watching climate change unfold
and helping us to mitigate that."
Some of these new cooling technologies
are more feasible than others.
But they all have the same goal:
Making AI use less water and energy.
But that might not be enough.
It could also make sense
to reduce the need
for cooling in the first place.
Something as simple
as shifting workloads
to cooler hours or water-secure regions
can significantly reduce
environmental impact.
There's also a school of thought in AI
called 'small data, big tasks,'
This approach relies
on changing the way
we train AI models altogether
by teaching it in small, deliberate steps –
rather than brute-force programming them
with millions of possible scenarios.
This reduces the massive computations
that keep data centers running
hot and thirsty around the clock.
And then we, the users of AI,
can also play our part.
You can opt for less water-intensive LLMs
or select which model they use
when entering prompts.
Another solution, of course,
is choosing not to use a chatbot
to draft every email
or automate every moment of friction
in our lives.
Because behind every "quick" AI response
is something very physical:
Water, energy,
and communities already stretched thin.
What about you?
How often do you use AI tools
like Claude or ChatGPT?
And are you concerned about
how much water and energy they use?
Let us know down in the comments
and visit dw.com for more.

OpenAI doesn't publicly share exactly how much energy or water its models consume, but one researcher we spoke to put together some estimates. For the simplest tasks, like rewording your email, their popular GPT-5 model uses as much electricity as it takes to power a three watt LED light bulb for around 20 minutes, and a little less than a teaspoon of water. For one person and one prompt it may not seem like much. Open AI says it receives 2.5 billion queries a day. Add those up and at the very least you're looking at enough water to meet the daily drinking needs of all the people in Monterey, Mexico, and enough energy to binge watch Netflix for more than 3000 years. The number of AI users is expected to roughly double over the next half decade, and as AI usage is increasing, so too is the number of data centers needed to support it. That's why researchers are racing to find new and better ways to keep server racks cool, like sinking data centers deep into the ocean and even launching them into space. Cooling has always been a core part of how data centers work. Even one of the first computers in the 1940s had vacuum tubes, which were cooled by fans and ventilation. Despite being literally trillions of times more powerful, the data centers of today still look kind of similar. The big difference is they're using a lot more energy. Nearly 50% of their entire energy use can go towards keeping the CPUs, that process your prompts, also known as microchips, from overheating. Google's hyperscaler in The Dalles, Oregon uses nearly a third of its city's water supply in 2021 according to public records obtained by the state's largest newspaper. Relying on water for cooling is a common approach. That's why when you look closely at this map, you see big clusters of data centers here close to freshwater. Nearly a 10th of the world's data centers are in this part of the US. The region is called the Great Lakes and is home to one fifth of the world's surface freshwater, much of it naturally cold. Using it lets data centers spend far less electricity on cooling. The water gets sprayed into rooftop cooling towers, allowed to cool naturally through evaporation and then used to keep servers from overheating. Fewer fans and less electricity. The method is called evaporative cooling. The tradeoff? A lot more water. "Less than 1% of the water in the Great Lakes is annually renewed and many ecosystems, city drinking water, industries rely on a relatively stable level of the lakes, so the water that can be taken out for use by humans – it has to fit within that water budget." Now let's look at the other side of this tradeoff. Google's data center in Mesa, Arizona, sits in one of the driest regions in the United States. So they went with air cooling: Giant fans pushing air across the servers. No water, but a lot more electricity. "The data centers in Ireland are consuming a fifth of all of our electricity." Rosie Leonard is an environmental activist from Ireland. "So it's really, really high. This circulates across metal plates - typically made from copper or aluminum - stuck onto the chips. Companies say using liquid to absorb heat from chips in a recirculating, closed-loop system, cools more effectively than chilling the air. "Liquids move heat faster than air does... subject to Irish and EU privacy laws and
the US warrant couldn't apply to data
stored in another nation. So tomorrow,
if our critical data is stored abroad,

during a war, if India's digital
backbone relies on foreign servers, our
systems from Aadhaar to UPI to defense
logistics could be shut down overnight
by foreign forces. So the moral of the
story is that a nation that doesn't own
its data cannot own its destiny. That is
why even the Reserve Bank of India
already mandates that payment system
data be stored only within India. And
you know, in spite of producing 20% of
the world's data, India barely stores 3%
of its own. So we cannot afford to give
this leverage away to anyone. So this
begs the question, if we cannot avoid
data centers, if we are running short on
water and if we don't want our citizens
to suffer, what do we do? Firstly, the
government must push all major companies
to use sustainable sources of water so
that we don't risk the water security of
our citizens. For example, Amazon plans
to use recycled water to cool more than
120 US data centers by 2030. This alone
will save over 530 million gallons of
fresh water annually. So the government
of India needs to ensure that these
companies follow sustainable methods
from day one and not after the crisis
hits. Secondly, India must go bullish on
renewable energy whether that is solar,
hydro or wind. We need to quickly build
capacity so that as global energy demand
explodes, we don't find ourselves at the
crossroad of development and energy
security. And lastly, the government
must ensure that development always
happens to improve the lives of the
citizens and not to destroy them.
Because the problem with unchecked
capitalism is that it often compromises
life for cash flow when the very purpose
of cash flow is to create a better life.

The first data center was actually just

room, and it was called the electronic
numerical integrator and computer, ENIAC

much smaller and more people had access
to them, which meant more people were
connecting to servers. We don't need to

buildings have also changed.
This is a CPU, and it's what a data
center used to run on. But as the
internet has moved beyond emails,

as you can tell, is much bigger,
requires much more power, and runs a lot
hotter. Thus, the fans. The change from
CPUs to GPUs is what's prompted a lot of
the problems that we're seeing today. We
have to use GPUs because they're the
only thing powerful enough to compute
all the things that AI needs to run,
which is why these buildings are so big.
And this is a small GPU that's really
not even made for AI stuff. But even
comparing it to this other one I have,
you can see the size and space required
just increases as you add more power.
Which brings me to the problem of today,

might feel a little random right now,

everything happening across all of your

important to have a monthly budget

how easy Monarch is to understand. It's
as simple as opening the dashboard with

Thank you again to Monarch. Now, we need
to go somewhere.
Electricity is the lifeblood of our

think people would know what to do if it
just turned off tomorrow. And because
we're so dependent on it, we notice when
the price goes up. And it's no secret
that everything in our lives is getting
more expensive than ever before. But
electricity specifically is
skyrocketing, especially if you're near
a data center. So, let's see what's
happening.
This is one of Facebook or Meta's data
centers outside of Dallas, Texas.
Altogether, these buildings are over 2
and 1/2 million square feet, and it cost
over 1 and 1/2 billion dollars to build.
But I want to focus on this section
right here. This is a power substation
and these power lines are the same ones
that eventually connect to your home,
which is why they build these data
centers near homes. Residential areas
already have a massive amount of power
being supplied to them compared to areas
like the middle of the desert. So, it's
cheaper for technology companies to just
buy land in a residential area to build
their data centers and then just hook up
to an already high output power grid.
But the closer these data centers get to
your home, the more expensive your power
gets. Which brings me to how electricity
prices are even set.
These are the examples of the different
types of power. Each one can create
electricity at a different price.
In this scenario, wind is the cheapest
at $20 per megawatt hour and the peaker
plant is the most expensive at $150 per
megawatt hour. It's not super important
to know what a peaker plant is for this
demonstration. They're basically a
safety net for the power grid. They only
power on when they're desperately
needed, usually in extreme cold or
extreme heat and they really only run
for about 300 to 500 hours per year. And
that's all you really need to know
today. But the way you and I get our
electricity price is through demand
based pricing. What that means is if the
demand for electricity today only
required these three sources of power,
then the price would be $30.
The suppliers of low gas would basically
break even and solar and wind would be
turning a profit. But as the demand
increases, the power companies need to
buy more power from higher up on the
supply chain. And as you go higher like
this,
your cost goes from $30 to
$80 per megawatt hour. It doesn't matter
if you're a business in a strip mall, a
person at home, or a 2.5 million square
foot data center. We all pay the same
price based on the demand. And if you
haven't caught on, those data centers
create a lot of demand. This whole
process that we just outlined is called
a capacity auction and it's run by the
electrical grid providers. One of those
providers is PJM and they service over
67 million people. The problem is last
summer they did their annual auction to
set prices for the 2026 through 27 year
and thanks to the data center demand the
prices went from $270 to $330
per megawatt day. To put that into
perspective the price for 2024 through
2025 was just $29.
So needless to say there is a real
reason to be concerned but electricity
isn't the only thing these data centers
use. Remember this? These are the fans
and without it this GPU wouldn't last
very long. But even with 2.5 million
square feet there's not enough room for
this many fans on every single GPU in a
data center. So instead of fans they
turn to something else.
Let's pretend that this is a rack of
GPUs at a data center. It's running 24
hours a day 7 days a week 365 days a
year. But on its own without those fans
it will eventually melt which is where
this comes in.
The steam you see here leaving the pot
is the heat going up through the water.
And if I keep pouring cold water into
the pot it will eventually completely
cool it down even if the heat is still
running. And the same thing happens in a
data center. These white tanks are water
tanks and these things right here are
water coolers. In this setup the water
tank serves as a backup reservoir and
then there are other water lines that
are running directly into the building
that are the same water lines that you
and I fill our bathtubs with. Which is a
problem on the surface because these
data centers use 130 million gallons of
water a year which is more than an
entire small city. And this all sounds
overwhelming at first but when you
actually start digging into it you start
to realize something. A lot of the
headlines lately would have you believe
that AI is the reason for all of this
water usage, but I want to show you
something.
This is how much water you'll use to
watch a 10-minute YouTube video. And
this is how much water you'll use
scrolling social media for an hour.
Based on that, you might think that AI
uses significantly more water than
either of these two. After all, the GPUs
for AI are way stronger, run way hotter,
and we need way more of them. So, it's
safe to assume that it's going to be a
lot of water. But surprisingly, this is
how much water is used by a single AI
prompt.
Which reveals the nuance to this entire
story about water. It's easy to point
and say that we're draining the world's
water just for AI, but no one wants to
look at how much water it takes to
scroll on TikTok or FaceTime a friend.
Now, don't get me wrong, 130 million
gallons of water is still an insane
amount to use, but that's why there's so
many new startups that are coming up
with ways to solve the water cooling
issue. Some new centers are using
non-potable water, otherwise known as
sewer water, to cool their buildings,
and other companies are even looking at
putting data centers on the ocean floor
or even up in space. But there's still
another problem in the shadows.
This is a chart of the monthly spend
[music] on new data centers. As you can
see, in 2021, it was less than $500
million
per month. Now, in 2025,
we've reached over $4.2
billion
per month, because that's what's needed
to build two to three of these a week.
But where is the money coming from? You
have to remember, these buildings don't
make any money until they're up and
running. And the AI companies can't run
their AI until these buildings are done.
So, it's reasonable to assume that
they're getting their money from
somewhere to fund the projects. And that
somewhere is the wild west of private
credit. Private credit can be as complex
as you want it to be, but to put it
simply, it's companies with a ton of
money that lend it out to other
companies. They're not banks and they
don't have to follow banking regulations
and they also don't need to disclose
anything to the public because they're
private. Over the next 3 years, it's
expected that $3 trillion is needed to
meet the build-out for AI demand. Of
that $3 trillion, half of it is expected
to come from public and private
institutions [music] and half of that
$1.5 trillion is expected to come from
private credit. [music] That means $750
billion will be lent out by private
credit to these AI companies to build
more data centers. But, the problem is
the entire private credit market is
worth about $3 trillion in total, which
means over the next few years they will
need to move over 25% of their entire
business into data center financing,
which won't be easy because a lot of
investors have no interest in being part
of private credit at the moment. Some of
the biggest private credit firms in the
world are actually blocking their
investors from withdrawing their funds
because the loans they wrote are
starting to default and the promise of
high returns are starting to disappear,
which leaves me wondering if they'll
even be able to support the data center
growth that they're projecting. But, if
everything is so negative and data
centers are just destroying our lives,
why do we keep doing it? Similar to a
lot of things in life, there isn't one
right answer. There are pros and cons to
everything and it's on us to decide what
we want to support. Outside of driving
electricity and water prices up, data
centers do bring in millions in new
jobs, tax revenue, and infrastructure.
Every time a new data center breaks
ground, it will bring in thousands of
new construction workers and effectively
jump-start these small rural towns that
have been left behind for years. Not
only that, but they're allowing people
who grow up in these small towns to stay
in these small towns and earn a good
living doing it. In fact, a data center
technician can make up to $113,000
per year and that new money gets spent
at local restaurants, saved in the local
banks, and it really does bring these
small towns back to life. But, on the
flip side, we've seen this before. When
the manufacturing companies came into
these small towns, built factories,
provided jobs, and then left them to
crumble when overseas manufacturing was
cheaper. So, as with everything, it's a
fine line. And on the electricity issue
specifically, these data centers aren't
going to just pull power infinitely from
the main grid. Between new regulations
and advancements in technology, they're
finding new ways to supply their own
power and run their buildings more
efficiently. And because of the increase
in demand due to these data centers, the
power companies have been forced to
upgrade their grid and expand their
current power supply, which does have a
positive impact on us at home.
When we zoom out, this can all be boiled
down to what I like to call the tech
loop.
New technology is created and we start
using it. As we use it more and more,
the tech companies realize it's working,
so they scale it.
But then, when they scale it, there's
usually some sort of issue, so we start
to complain. In this case, electricity
and water prices. But, the only problem
is is we're all complaining, but we're
still using the new technology, so the
companies continue to scale. And when
they continue to scale, they release new
tech products, and the cycle continues
over and over again. And the only way to
truly escape this loop from happening is
to stop using the technology itself,
which I don't really see happening at
this point. '90s, people thought that
the internet was going to collapse
society, but now we use it every day,
which is another reminder that all of
these things that we see in the
headlines and we talk about every day
have so much more beneath the surface
when you actually look at what's going
on. There's usually some spin, bias, or
false statements made to make you feel a
certain way. And that's why it's so
important to get a clear understanding
of what's actually happening. This video
was a really tricky one to make, mainly
because of how exaggerated and overblown
a lot of the headlines about data
centers are. Don't get me wrong, there
is 100% a problem with the electricity
and water prices increasing, but I do
think it's important to understand the
nuance behind every situation [music]
because it's not always cut and dry.
Similar to the financial tools that we
normally discuss, if you go in with the
right information, you can usually play
the game and end up coming out on top.
But, if you go in blindly and just trust
the marketing or the headlines, you
usually get stuck in the trap. I hope
you enjoyed my arts and crafts this week
and got something out of this setup. It
took longer than I would like to admit
to build, but I do think it added a lot
to the video. Potentially, Grantopia or
Grantville will come back in a future
video. If you'd like to see that, make
sure you subscribe for more because like
I've said in every video, we are looking
to level up in every way with these
videos and I want to bring you truly
unique ideas and formats and
explanations so that you can get the
most out of the time you spend on
YouTube. Make sure to subscribe if
that's something you want to be a part
of, and if you're looking for something
to watch next, YouTube thinks you might
like this one. Let's see if they're
right.

It's easy to point and say that we're draining the world's water just for AI, but no one wants to look at how much water it takes to scroll on TikTok or FaceTime a friend. Now, don't get me wrong, 130 million gallons of water is still an insane amount to use, but that's why there's so many new startups that are coming up with ways to solve the water cooling issue. Some new centers are using non-potable water, otherwise known as sewer water, to cool their buildings, and other companies are even looking at putting data centers on the ocean floor or even up in space. But there's still another problem in the shadows. This is a chart of the monthly spend [music] on new data centers. As you can see, in 2021, it was less than $500 million per month. Now, in 2025, we've reached over $4.2 billion per month, because that's what's needed to build two to three of these a week. But where is the money coming from? You have to remember, these buildings don't make any money until they're up and running. And the AI companies can't run their AI until these buildings are done. So, it's reasonable to assume that they're getting their money from somewhere to fund the projects. And that somewhere is the wild west of private credit. Private credit can be as complex as you want it to be, but to put it simply, it's companies with a ton of money that lend it out to other companies. They're not banks and they don't have to follow banking regulations and they also don't need to disclose anything to the public because they're private. Over the next 3 years, it's expected that $3 trillion is needed to meet the build-out for AI demand. Of that $3 trillion, half of it is expected to come from public and private institutions [music] and half of that $1.5 trillion is expected to come from private credit. [music] That means $750 billion will be lent out by private credit to these AI companies to build more data centers. But, the problem is the entire private credit market is worth about $3 trillion in total, which means over the next few years they will need to move over 25% of their entire business into data center financing, which won't be easy because a lot of investors have no interest in being part of private credit at the moment. Some of the biggest private credit firms in the world are actually blocking their investors from withdrawing their funds because the loans they wrote are starting to default and the promise of high returns are starting to disappear, which leaves me wondering if they'll even be able to support the data center growth that they're projecting. But, if everything is so negative and data centers are just destroying our lives, why do we keep doing it? Similar to a lot of things in life, there isn't one right answer. There are pros and cons to everything and it's on us to decide what we want to support. Outside of driving electricity and water prices up, data centers do bring in millions in new jobs, tax revenue, and infrastructure. Every time a new data center breaks ground, it will bring in thousands of new construction workers and effectively jump-start these small rural towns that have been left behind for years. Not only that, but they're allowing people who grow up in these small towns to stay in these small towns and earn a good living doing it. In fact, a data center technician can make up to $113,000 per year and that new money gets spent at local restaurants, saved in the local banks, and it really does bring these small towns back to life. But, on the flip side, we've seen this before. When the manufacturing companies came into these small towns, built factories, provided jobs, and then left them to crumble when overseas manufacturing was cheaper. So, as with everything, it's a fine line. And on the electricity issue specifically, these data centers aren't going to just pull power infinitely from the main grid. Between new regulations and advancements in technology, they're finding new ways to supply their own power and run their buildings more efficiently. And because of the increase in demand due to these data centers, the power companies have been forced to upgrade their grid and expand their current power supply, which does have a positive impact on us at home. When we zoom out, this can all be boiled down to what I like to call the tech loop. New technology is created and we start using it. As we use it more and more, the tech companies realize it's working, so they scale it. But then, when they scale it, there's usually some sort of issue, so we start to complain. In this case, electricity and water prices. But, the only problem is is we're all complaining, but we're still using the new technology, so the companies continue to scale. And when they continue to scale, they release new tech products, and the cycle continues over and over again. system, but instead evaporates [music]
and disappears from the local cycle.
That's because it's not reused in a

towers where it absorbs heat from
servers and escapes as vapor.
>> In the coming years, the electricity
demand of AI optimized [music] data
centers could increase by up to 300%,
also raising their indirect water
footprint.
>> Today, data centers make up about 4% of
US electricity demand. In just the next
3 years, that's expected to triple. AI
uses enormous amounts of water and with
constant [music] evaporation it leaves
an increasingly visible impact on our
resources. So how AI is wiping out our
drinking water? Artificial intelligence
is often perceived as something
intangible. However, [music] the data
centers that support it already account
for nearly 2% of global electricity
consumption. Every response generated by
an advanced model depends on an
infrastructure that operates
continuously. Behind every query are
massive data centers [music] full of
servers that produce constant heat. Some
racks exceed 30 to 50 kilowatts of
thermal power so that heat must be
continuously [music] dissipated to
prevent failures. To achieve this, large
volumes of water are used, [music] often
portable. In certain facilities,
consumption can reach up to 4 million
gallons per day. Therefore, beyond
electricity consumption and carbon
emissions, AI also has a significant
water footprint. Understanding this
dimension is crucial, especially if the
energy demand of AI linked data centers
could increase by up to 400%. But then
at the same time, somehow a Morgan
Stanley projection predicted annual
water use for cooling and electricity
generation by AI data centers could
reach around a,000 billion L by 2028.
>> First, direct [music] water consumption
occurs within the data centers
themselves. Traditionally, many of these
buildings used [music] air cooling
systems, a method that still represents
around 60% of older facilities. However,
as AI models have become larger and more
demanding, the energy density of server
racks has [music] increased
significantly. Today, some systems
exceed 40 kW per rack in highdensity
facilities, making conventional
ventilation insufficient. As a result,
[music] liquid cooling systems have been
implemented. These systems circulate a
coolant over or near the chips,
absorbing heat and transferring it to
[music] heat exchangers. There the heat
is transferred to water which in certain
large-scale centers can consume more
than 3 million L daily directed to
cooling towers. In these centers, fans
and evaporation reduce [music] the water
temperature to restart the cycle. The
crucial factor is that the water used
must [music] be clean, often portable or
treated to very high standards. This
water is taken from the water supply
[music] because it has to be clean fresh
water to avoid clogs or bacteria growth.
>> This is a technical requirement as
impure water can cause corrosion and
reduce system efficiency. Therefore, it
is not just any water but a resource
that in many regions accounts for less
[music] than 3% of available water
competing with human and agricultural
consumption. Additionally, a significant
portion of the water is lost through
evaporation. When hot water reaches the
towers, a fraction turns into vapor with
losses in large facilities exceeding
[music] 500,000 L daily. This water does
not return to the immediate local
system. This has made water scarcity a
real risk for the operation of these
data centers. [music]
>> Meanwhile, as data centers continue to
mushroom, we will need more and more
water. So, this is not just an
environmental problem, it's also a
technological one because without water,
there is no AI. Direct consumption is
[music] not the only dimension of the
problem. The indirect water footprint
can be even larger. To understand it, it
is necessary to look beyond the data
center building and observe how the
electricity that powers it is generated.
Electricity generation, especially in
coal, gas or nuclear plants, can sustain
facilities demanding more than 100
megawatts of continuous power. These
plants heat water to produce steam that
drives turbines and generates
electricity. Later that steam must be
cooled which again involves water
consuming cooling systems. In
conventional thermal plants electricity
production can involve average water
consumption of around 2 L per kowatt
hour generated. As AI data centers
increase their energy demand pressure on
power plants also rises. [music] If
demand grows by 400% by 2030 the water
impact could be amplified in regions
reliant on thermal sources. Worldwide
data center water consumption is
increasing drastically and is expected
to more than double by 2030.
>> Furthermore, this problem continues into
the semiconductor manufacturing stage.
[music] The industry accounts for nearly
20% of ultra pure water consumption in
some Asian tech hubs. The chips that
power advanced models do not appear
spontaneously. [music] Their production
involves extremely delicate processes
that use ultra pure water to clean
silicon wafers and remove microscopic
particles. Each wafer may go through
more than 1,000 cleaning and chemical
treatment [music] steps before becoming
a functional chip. Producing a single
advanced chip can require thousands of
lers of water in different industrial
[music] phases. Moreover, producing
ultra pure water requires even more
fresh water as an initial input. In some
complexes, total consumption can exceed
10 million L per day, further expanding
the indirect water footprint of
artificial intelligence. In just one
day, the average data center could use
300,000 gallons of water to cool itself.
The same water consumption as a 100,000
homes. Water is involved at multiple
levels, and extracting raw materials can
require more than 15,000 L per ton.
[music] AI does not just consume water
when answering a question. It uses water
throughout the entire chain that makes
it possible. This scenario has generated
social tensions in various regions.
[music] In water stressed areas,
installing data centers has sparked
protests where availability drops below
1,700 cubic meters per person per year.
Residents question whether it is
sustainable [music] to allocate large
volumes of water to digital
infrastructure when irrigation or
domestic [music] use is restricted. In
the United States, the debate focuses on
balancing technological expansion with
water scarcity [music] and protecting
underground water resources. They went
through this process called dewatering
because of Amazon's data center
construction where [music] they were
actually removing groundwater that
people rely on here for their drinking
water.
>> In [music] response to these criticisms,
major tech companies have committed to
water neutrality, aiming to replenish up
to 120% of consumed water. That is
returning to the environment an amount
of water equal to or greater than what
they [music] consume. They are
implementing restoration and efficiency
projects. And some centers already
recycle up to 85% of water used. At the
same time, technical solutions are being
explored such as immersion cooling,
locating centers in cold regions, and
using the ocean as a natural heat sink.
Residual heat is also being reused for
urban heating with projects supplying
more than 10,000 homes in certain
European cities. However, these
initiatives still represent a limited
portion of global infrastructure. For
example, Microsoft builds data centers
in Canada to take advantage of the cold
climate and naturally cool servers.
>> So, one of the great things about
building in Canada and in colder
climates is that we can just use free
air cooling from outside air
temperatures.
>> Ultimately, the relationship between
artificial intelligence and drinking
water is not anecdotal but structural.
And the digital sector could reach 8%
[music]
of global electricity consumption. From
training a model to answering a query,
electricity, semiconductors, [music]
thermal systems, and water are
constantly involved. Each increase in
processing capacity can double energy
demand in [music] just a few years. And
each expansion of AI raises questions in
a context where more than 2 billion
people already struggle to access
sufficient water.
>> Increasingly, these data centers are
being built in areas where water is
scarce, like Mexico. Consequently, the
challenge is not only to make models
[music] faster or more accurate, but
also to design them to require less
energy and water. Artificial
intelligence promises to transform the
economy, science, and everyday life.
However, its sustainable future will
depend on how a resource as essential as
water is managed.
>> At Economy Media, your opinion matters
to us. Subscribe and let us know what

segments.text: As we've told you, Wednesday is Earth
Day.
For some, it may feel hard to celebrate
given recent efforts to increase our
reliance on coal and oil.
But, innovators keep coming up with new
technologies for a cleaner world.
And this morning, David Pogue introduces
us to a few of them.
Here are two global problems you
probably don't like to think about.
First, burning gas and coal is still
heating up the planet. Second, you know
those thousands of AI data centers
popping up all over the country?
They consume enormous amounts of power,
which produces even more carbon
pollution and drives up our electric
bills. And this is not really going to
stop. And we're still at the beginning
of this demand. Garth Sheldon-Coulson is
the CEO and co-founder of Pelamis. He
hopes to address both problems at once
with wave energy. The ocean is really
unlimited in terms of how much energy is
available. It will really be the
cheapest energy on the planet. What this
thing is really is a floating
hydroelectric dam.
Pelamis' test model, the Ocean 2, now
rests on its side at the company's plant
in Vancouver, Washington. As it goes up
and down with the waves,
it causes water that's in that tube to
be forced up into the top. Okay.
>> Once it's in the ball, the water is
forced through a turbine.
The turbine spins, and that's what makes
the electricity. The water sits here.
So, all you see is this part on top.
Using a model of the latest design, the
Ocean 3, Sheldon-Coulson explained the
most surprising part. There's no anchor
and no cables. And this is not tethered
to the bottom? Correct. And this is a
self-propelled system. And so, it's like
a little Roomba, except it's
[clears throat] enormous. There's no
cable to bring power back to shore,
either. In essence, these are floating
data centers. They generate power from
the waves, process AI computing tasks on
the spot, and send us the answers by
satellite. When you deploy many of our
systems, they work together basically as
a data center. So, we think of it as a
really good alternative to data centers
on land.
Pelamis has all of the private funding
it needs because it offers AI companies
a quicker, cleaner way to get power than
building data centers on land.
Construction of the Ocean 3s is well
underway.
When will this exact unit see water?
Uh this system will be deployed around
August of this year. And what are the
advantages of the power that it will
generate? Clean, no fuel, no land use,
no getting in the way of other
activities on land, and and very fast to
scale because we can just manufacture
the systems.
Eventually, the company hopes to deploy
thousands of them far out at sea. Do you
ever wake up in the morning going, "I
might be the guy who can solve two of
the biggest problems in the world, not
enough energy and too much pollution?"
It is really exciting that we're working
on something that is coming along right
at the right time in a way that's much
cleaner, much more sustainable, and
quite scalable so that we can really
meet that demand as it comes.

without being judged or we want to draft

But did you know that one Chad GPT

the electricity compared to a Google

using AI for our aid is coming at a
price.
Let me explain. In short, artificial
intelligence trains and runs on
resources such as water and electricity
and so it puts pressure on the
environment. To give you context, let me
walk you through the process of using an

or query in its database and this data
lives in servers and supercomputers
placed in data centers. This is what a
supercomput looks like. So my prompt
travels from my phone or computer to the
database and the devices there quickly
rumage through all the information
relevant to my search and send back an
answer to the chatbot. The AI assistant
then throws its response at me. And when
the devices were processing large
amounts of data to bring me one answer,
they were also heating up because I'm
not the only one looking for answers,

are feeding prompts into AI assistants
every day. Oh, Chachi BT, I am so sorry
to bother you, but could you please

[Music]
All these searches make servers and
supercomputers heat up. Think of it like
your phone or laptop and how sometimes
they heat up when you open too many apps
or tabs. To run quick searches,
supercomputers need chips called
graphics processing units or GPUs, which
can process large amounts of information
in a short period. According to the
United Nations Environment Program, the
microchips that power AI need rare earth
elements, which are often mined in
environmentally destructive ways. And to
build a single 2 kg computer, 800 kg of
raw material is required. According to a
digital economy report by the United
Nations Conference on Trade and
Development, the buildings or data
centers where the servers and
supercomputers are placed need cooling
systems to avoid overheating. The data
centers also produce electronic waste
and use a lot of water, which UNEP said
is becoming scarce in many places. Water
makes up 71% of the Earth's surface, but
only less than 1% of it is fit for
consumption because the rest of it is
either salt water, frozen or
inaccessible due to being too far under
the ground. And the less than 1% of
fresh water that we need for human
consumption is also used by industries
of agriculture and manufacturing. Why do
data centers use water? One, to generate
electricity and two, as liquid coolant
to combat the heat generated by servers
and equipment. According to NPR, a
midsized data center uses 11 lakh lers
of water in a
[Music]
day. A few biggies in the tech field
have spoken on this. Meta said they're
stemming their environmental footprint
by matching their energy use with
renewable energy, meaning they add new
wind and solar projects to local grids,
including those where their data centers
are located. Pretty excited about this
latest update to Meta AI. It basically
lets you generate images of yourself in
any style and doing anything that you
want. On water, Meta said their goal is
restoring more water than they consume
globally in 2030. Meta AI is on track to
reach our goal of becoming the most used
AI assistant in the world by the end of
the year. Microsoft made a similar
pledge in 2020. In 2021, Google
announced a water stewardship program
aiming to replenish 120% of the
freshwater volume they consume. In 2024,
the company announced it would comply
with stricter environmental needs and
revise a data center project in
droughtstricken South American country
Chile after a local court there
temporarily revoked permissions for the
project. Statista reported that there
are at least
10,332 data centers across the world
with US having the most at more than
5,000. There were 3,600 data centers in
2015 and in 10 years the number has gone
up by about 7,000. This would give you
an idea of the scale at which data
centers are proliferating. A World
Economic Forum report said Microsoft's
carbon emissions saw a 30% jump since
2020 and Google's carbon emissions saw a
50% jump because of data center
expansion. Data centers need a lot of
electricity to keep running. One Chat
GPT search is said to use up nearly 10
times the electricity compared to a
simple Google search. Why is that
though? again because an AI assistant
processes large amounts of information
and frames conversational sentences to
respond to users. A Google search on the
other hand just throws up links which
the users are required to visit so they
can figure out answers to their
questions. For example, I asked Grog AI
and Google the same question. What's a
quick remedy to get rid of a pimple?
Well, Google directed me to these links.
Groke AI straight up wrote me a remedy.
Now, even Google has added an AI
overview feature which sums up the
search. Aside from giving links,
Bloomberg said the world's data centers
could use as much electricity for a year
as could power the whole of Italy or
Australia. The demand for electricity
due to an AI surge is now outstripping
the available power supply in many parts
of the world, Bloomberg said. According
to DW, the carbon dioxide emissions from
training a single AI model are more than
five times the emissions a car generates
in its lifetime. Most of the energy or
electricity used for data centers comes
from fossil fuels such as coal and gas.
Burning these creates carbon emissions
and contributes to climate change. A
Goldman Sachs report said the carbon
dioxide emissions of data centers may
more than double between 2022 and 2030.
What we're seeing right now is an AI
explosion. Tech is evolving. Our parents
didn't have search engines to look up
stuff in real time. Then we got the
internet and Google literally became a
verb because of how much we started
using it. Posts became emails and
traditional phone calls turned to
realtime texts and calls. Then AI came
into the picture and everyone can send
prompts left, right, and center. It's
become clearer that the next generation
of services requires building full
general intelligence. Building the best
AI assistants, AI for creators, AI for
businesses, and more. That needs
advances in every area of AI. And as
with anything new, it is not without
challenges. Few hundred thousand people
die on the roads due to car accidents.
And we take that as a given. But if one
person is killed by an autonomous car,
the provider of that has to go back to
the drawing board for 2 years. In other
words, because the bar we have of
expectations of machines is at a
different level than of human beings,
this becomes actually very difficult
when you try to adopt at scale. So
adopting AI at scale is hard work and
will continue to be. So the conversation
around pollution generated by AI is
evolving. There's also a parallel
conversation about AI helping the
environment and combating climate
change. For example, Myrose Group, a
Norwegian oil spill detection system, is
using AI to detect oil spills around
offshore installations to help find out
if the water is contaminated. So we do a
automatic 24/7 detection that would give
the the operators an alert that there is
a probable oil spill taking place and
they would need to look into it and take
their required action to cope with it.
South Korea is using AI powered bins to
identify almost all kinds of plastic
bottles available in the country within
seconds and sort them out for recycling.
Scientists are using AI to track
deforestation in real time, track
weather patterns, and assist with more
efficient waste management. Experts
think that by 2030, AI might help cut
down global emissions by 10%. The key is
then to bring a balance between the two
poles of AI usage, the pollution it
causes and the pollution it helps stem.
While energy and water use by AI is a
concern as of now, we'll have to wait
and see how companies navigate this in
the future and whether they're able to
mitigate the problem.

There's also a parallel conversation about AI helping the environment and combating climate change. For example, Myrose Group, a Norwegian oil spill detection system, is using AI to detect oil spills around offshore installations to help find out if the water is contaminated. So we do a automatic 24/7 detection that would give the the operators an alert that there is a probable oil spill taking place and they would need to look into it and take their required action to cope with it. South Korea is using AI powered bins to identify almost all kinds of plastic bottles available in the country within seconds and sort them out for recycling. Scientists are using AI to track deforestation in real time, track weather patterns, and assist with more efficient waste management. Experts think that by 2030, AI might help cut down global emissions by 10%. The key is then to bring a balance between the two poles of AI usage, the pollution it causes and the pollution it helps stem. While energy and water use by AI is a concern as of now, we'll have to wait and see how companies navigate this in the future and whether they're able to mitigate the problem.

segments.text: That's the question we set out to answer recently.
We analysed brand new data sets
and consulted extensively
with the tech sector,
the energy industry, policymakers and international experts.
Most of AI's energy needs are concentrated in data centres
and inside each data centre are any number of servers,
which create immense amounts of heat as they process data.
Running those servers and keeping them
cool is one of the main drivers of electricity consumption for AI.

To give you an idea of how much
electricity these data centres use,

today, a hyperscale data centre
operated by a big tech company
uses about as much electricity as 100,000 households.
The largest data centre currently under development
will use 20 times that amount.
Global investment in data
centres has nearly doubled since 2022, and in 2024,
it amounted to half $1 trillion.
That boom in investment
has led to growing concerns
about the possibility of skyrocketing electricity demand
to power these data centres.
On a global scale, data centres accounted for about 1.5%
of electricity consumption in 2024.
By 2030, electricity consumption
by data centres is set to roughly double
to about 3% of global consumption.
That means data centres will be consuming around
945 terawatt hours a year.
And to put that into context,
that's about as much as the entire yearly electricity consumption
of Japan today.
The processing needs of AI will be the most significant driver
of this increase in data centre electricity consumption.
Regardless of where you're interacting with AI,
it's highly likely that the data will be routed through one
of a few clusters of data centres.
They're dotted all around the globe,
but a few markets are dominant today.
The United States accounted for 45% of data centre
electricity consumption in 2024, followed by 25% in China
and 15% in Europe.

Over the next ten years, we see new data centers
in the pipeline all over the world,

but some countries are
set to see more growth than others,
which could have big effects on their electricity systems.
In the US,
data centres are on course to account
for almost half of the overall growth
in electricity demand between now and 2030.
Interestingly, the US
economy is set to consume more electricity in 2030
for processing data

than producing all aluminum,
steel, cement and chemicals combined.

And it's not the only region where the
effects could be pronounced.
In Japan, data centres could account
for more than half of demand growth,
and in Malaysia, it could be as much as one fifth.
Electricity grids are already under strain in many places,

and we estimate that around
20% of planned data centre projects
could be at risk of delays if these strains aren't addressed.
We've also found that 50% of data centres under development
in the United States, for example, are in places
where there are already large clusters of other data centres,
which could risk strains on local grids and
hold ups to them coming online.
These problems could be replicated in other places
where there are data centre clusters around the world.

Natural gas and renewables take
the lead in powering data centres.
Natural gas can provide baseload power in places where it is
cheap and abundant.
Notably the United States and the Middle East.
Renewables are cost effective, quick to build
and supported by many countries and companies.
In the next decade,
We also see the tech sector helping to bring forward
new nuclear and geothermal technologies as well,
such as small modular nuclear reactors.
There are significant uncertainties that remain,
but our analysis makes it very clear that because of AI,
the energy and tech sectors are now on a journey together.
The IEA will continue to analyse
what this means for the future of energy,
and we'll be back with more videos
exploring key findings from this report very soon.

houses rows of computers and equipment
for storing processing and transmitting
data they typically have few windows and
extra security including cameras and
sometimes biometric screening the
computers need a lot of electricity to
operate and to keep the equipment from
overheating that also requires more
electricity and huge amounts of water
for elaborate air
conditioning the International Energy
agency says one large Data Center uses
the same amount of electricity as it
takes to power 400,000 electric cars
tech companies are increasingly using
their data centers to train and operate
artificial intelligence systems training
an AI model to learn from huge amounts
of data requires additional computing
power conventional data center Services
run on processors known as
CPUs AI relies on Graphics processing
units or
gpus the iea estimates that asking open
AI gbt to compose an answer requires
nearly 10 times as much electricity as a
traditional Google
search Amazon Microsoft and Google have
all announced plans to tap into nuclear
energy to help power their us data
centers they've also helped finance new
wind and solar farms and other
alternative energy sources in the
near-term however most data centers are
still heavily reliant on the surrounding
electricity grid which is largely
powered by burning fossil fuels the
primary cause of global
warming this is making it harder for
some countries and tech companies to
keep their promises to address climate
change
